g.title: Fully Assembled Motion Ball Front View
g.desc: This front-view diagram shows the completed motion ball. It is a spherical cage made of interlocking 5mm laser-cut plywood pieces. A horizontal circular platform is centered inside, featuring etched 'X' and 'Y' axis arrows. Secured to the center of this platform with a small brass screw is a rectangular black electronic sensor board with a white connector. Four colored wires (red, white, yellow, and black) extend from this connector to the left. The outer spherical shape is formed by four vertical semicircular ribs that notch into the central platform. Two thick black rubber bands are wrapped horizontally around the entire outer structure, one near the top and one near the bottom, to hold all the wooden pieces firmly together.

g.title: Motion Ball Kit Materials Inventory
g.desc: This inventory list for the Piper Make Motion Ball kit includes several wooden, electronic, and hardware pieces. The wooden parts consist of one flat 'Center Base', four 'Sides', and two 'Caps' that will feel like smooth 5mm thick plywood. The hardware includes two very small metal '1/4-inch Screws' and two thick, stretchy rubber 'O-Rings'. The electronics include one small, rectangular 'Sensor' board with tiny raised components and one flexible '4-Pin Cable' with plastic connectors at both ends.

g.title: Gather and Organize Your Initial Supplies
g.desc: This component guide shows seven types of parts to lay out. Plywood pieces include: one large 'Center Base' circle with four large inner cutouts and four outer edge notches; two small 'Caps' that feel like four-leaf clovers with deep notches; and four 'Sides' shaped like arched rainbows with notches on both curves. Hardware and electronics include: two small, flexible rubber 'O-Rings'; two short 1/4 inch brass 'Screws'; one long, flat '4-Pin Cable' ribbon with a connector; and one small rectangular 'Sensor' board with two circular mounting holes on one side.

g.title: Large Circular Wooden Center Base Piece
g.desc: This single circular component is made of 5mm thick laser-cut plywood. It can be identified by four large, rounded-triangular cutouts that leave a solid plus-sign shape in the center of the piece. Along the outer circular edge, there are four small, evenly spaced U-shaped notches. Trace the outer rim to locate the notches and feel the interior to identify the large openings and central cross structure.

g.title: Two Notched Circular Wood Caps
g.desc: Locate two identical 5mm thick wooden pieces called Caps. Each is a flat circular disk. You can identify them by feeling for four deep, U-shaped notches cut into the outer edge. These notches are evenly spaced around the perimeter at the top, bottom, left, and right positions, giving the piece a symmetrical shape similar to a cross with rounded outer edges.

g.title: Four Semi-Circular Wood Side Pieces
g.desc: These are four identical 5mm thick wood pieces shaped like a wide arch. To identify them, feel the smooth inner curve for a single small tab pointing inward at the very center. On the outer curve, there are two deep U-shaped notches. Each end of the arch also features a small square notch, creating two 'feet' at the base.

g.title: Two Large Flexible Rubber O-Rings
g.desc: Identify two black rubber O-rings. These are stretchy, flexible rings approximately 2 inches in diameter with a smooth, squishy texture. They feel like small, thin donuts with a circular cross-section. Because they are perfectly symmetrical rings, they can be oriented in any direction.

g.title: Two Quarter-Inch Brass Screws
g.desc: Locate two small brass screws, each 1/4 inch long. Tactually, you can identify them by their smooth, circular head with a single slot across the middle and the short, ridged texture of the threaded shaft. These metal pieces will feel colder and heavier than the plywood parts.

g.title: 4-Pin Grove to Female Pin Cable
g.desc: Locate a single flat ribbon of four thin, flexible wires joined side-by-side. On one end, all four wires terminate in a single, small rectangular plastic plug that has a tactile notch on one side for alignment. On the other end, the four wires split apart, each ending in its own tiny, separate rectangular plastic connector.

g.title: Locate the Sensor Module
g.desc: Find the single sensor module. It's a small, flat, rectangular electronic board made of plastic, not wood. Feel for a slightly bumpy surface from its tiny electronic parts. On one of its shorter ends, you'll find a raised, rectangular plastic connector that cables plug into. Near this end, feel for two small, round screw holes on the long edges of the board.

g.title: Mount Motion Sensor to Circular Base
g.desc: Connect the white 4-pin cable to the matching white socket on the side of the small black motion sensor board. Place the sensor on the center of the large circular wooden base, which you can identify by its four large cutout sections. Align the two small holes on the sensor board with the two corresponding holes in the middle of the wooden base. Secure the sensor by inserting two brass screws through these holes and tightening them.

g.title: Attaching Side Ribs to the Circular Base
g.desc: This step shows four crescent-shaped wooden ribs being attached to the circular base. The base has a small, rectangular sensor board already fastened to its center with two brass screws. Each rib has a notch that fits into a slot on the edge of the base. Find the rib with a 'Z' axis engraving. Following the side view diagram, ensure the '+' symbol on this rib is oriented upwards, above the flat surface of the base, as you slot all four ribs into place.

g.title: Base Piece Z-Axis Orientation Side View
g.desc: A side-view diagram illustrating the vertical orientation of the flat plywood base. It defines the Z-axis: the top surface of the 5mm thick base is the positive direction (marked with a plus sign and upward arrow), and the bottom surface is the negative direction (marked with a minus sign and downward arrow).

g.title: Attaching rubber O-rings to the frame
g.desc: In step 3, locate two large, stretchy rubber O-rings. The wooden frame is a cage-like sphere made of interlocking curved pieces. Identify four small notches on the outer edges of the vertical arches near the top. Stretch one O-ring and pull it down over the top of the frame until it seats securely into these four notches. A second O-ring is shown already attached in a similar way to the notches on the bottom half of the frame.

g.title: Final View of the Completed Assembly
g.desc: The final assembly is a spherical structure made from several interlocking circular and semi-circular 5mm plywood pieces. Inside this wooden cage, a small rectangular black electronic sensor is mounted horizontally. The sensor has a white connector port with four colored wires attached. Black elastic bands are stretched across parts of the outer frame to secure the structure. The text 'ASSEMBLY COMPLETE!' confirms that all physical building steps are finished.
